title: Interesting Object, Curious Agent: Learning Task-Agnostic Exploration

paragraphs.text:
## **Interesting Object, Curious Agent: Learning Task-Agnostic Exploration**

**Simone Parisi**[1] _[∗]_ **Victoria Dean**[2] _[∗]_ **Deepak Pathak**[2] **Abhinav Gupta**[1] 1Facebook AI Research 2Carnegie Mellon University

## **Abstract**

Common approaches for task-agnostic exploration learn tabula-rasa –the agent assumes isolated environments and no prior knowledge or experience. However, in the real world, agents learn in many environments and always come with prior experiences as they explore new ones. Exploration is a lifelong process. In this paper, we propose a paradigm change in the formulation and evaluation of taskagnostic exploration. In this setup, the agent first _learns to explore_ across many environments without any extrinsic goal in a task-agnostic manner. Later on, the agent effectively transfers the learned _exploration policy_ to better explore new environments when solving tasks. In this context, we evaluate several baseline exploration strategies and present a simple yet effective approach to learning taskagnostic exploration policies. Our key idea is that there are two components of exploration: (1) an agent-centric component encouraging exploration of unseen parts of the environment based on an agent’s belief; (2) an environment-centric component encouraging exploration of inherently interesting objects. We show that our formulation is effective and provides the most consistent exploration across several training-testing environment pairs. We also introduce benchmarks and metrics for evaluating task-agnostic exploration strategies. The source code is available at `https://github.com/sparisi/cbet/` .

## **1 Introduction**

Exploration is one of the key unsolved problems in building intelligent agents capable of behaving like humans. In reinforcement learning (RL), exploration is usually studied under two different settings. The first is task-driven exploration, where the reward is well-defined and the agent’s goal is to explore in order to maximize long-term rewards. However, in real life, external rewards are either sparse or unknown altogether. In this setting, exploration is task-agnostic: given a new environment, the agent has to explore it in absence of any external reward. Common approaches to encourage task-agnostic exploration use intrinsically motivated rewards such as prediction curiosity [35, 47], empowerment [39], or visitation counts [4, 34]. But does this setup represent how humans explore?

We argue that the commonly-used task-agnostic exploration setup is unrealistic, both from practical and academic viewpoints. This setup assumes environments in isolation and agents exploring tabularasa, i.e., with no prior knowledge or experience. By contrast, we as humans do not learn from one environment in isolation and we do not throw away our past knowledge every time we encounter a new environment [14]. Exploration is rather a lifelong process: every time we encounter new environments, we use our prior knowledge and experience to develop new efficient exploration strategies. In this paper, we view the exploration problem from a continual learning lens. More specifically, in this setup, the learning agent interacts with one or many environments without any extrinsic goal. At this time, the agent _learns to explore_ the environments. Later on, the agent effectively transfers the learned _exploration policy_ to explore new environments, rather than exploring the new environment tabula-rasa.

> _∗_ Equal contribution. Contacts: `sparisi@fb.com` and `vdean@cmu.edu`

35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021).

Later, when tasked with reaching a box behind a door, the agent starts by picking up the key.

A key question in learning how to explore is what to learn and how to transfer prior knowledge from one environment to another. Most existing task-agnostic exploration approaches, such as visitation counts, curiosity, or empowerment, define intrinsic rewards in an _agent-centric_ manner: they encourage exploration of unseen parts of the environment based on the agent’s own belief. In these approaches, exploration is driven by what the agent knows about the world. However, most do not make a distinction between what the agent believes it is interested in and states that would make any agent interested. For example, if the agent uses a visitation count model and has seen many objects of one kind in one environment, it would not explore the same type of objects again in a new environment. This seems to be in stark contrast to how humans explore. Consider a switch with a bell sign. Even though we might have pressed hundreds of doorbell switches (and even this instance), we are still attracted to press it. Some objects in the world just demand curiosity. We argue that apart from an ‘agentcentric’ component, there is an ‘environment-centric’ component to exploration, which can be learned from prior knowledge and experiences.

In this paper, we propose a paradigm change to move

away from stand-alone isolated task-agnostic environment exploration to a more realistic multienvironment transfer-exploration setup[2] . We show how to learn exploration policies both from single- and multi-environment interaction, and how to transfer them to unseen environments. This transfer-exploration setup allows agents to use prior experiences for learning task-agnostic exploration. Notably, classic stand-alone task-agnostic approaches were designed for tabula-rasa exploration and hence only explore in an agent-centric manner. They fail to capture the inherent interestingness of some environment components. With this insight, we propose _Change-Based Exploration Transfer (CBET)_ , a simple yet effective approach learning joint agent-centric and environment-centric exploration. The key idea is for an agent to seek out both surprises (unseen areas) and high-impact (interesting) components of the environment. The experiments show that C-BET (a) learns more effectively when placed in a multi-environment setup, and (b) either outperforms or performs competitively with prior methods across several unseen testing environments. We hope this paper will inspire exploration research to focus more on learning from multiple environments and transferring experiences rather than tabula-rasa exploration.

## **2 Preliminaries and Related Work**

We consider environments governed by Markov Decision Processes (MDPs). In MDPs, an agent observes the state of the environment _s_ and selects actions _a_ according to a policy _π_ ( _a|s_ ). In turn, the environment changes, providing a new observation _s[′]_ and a reward _r_ . Through environment interaction, the agent collects episodes, i.e., sequences of states, actions and rewards ( _st, at, rt_ ) _t_ =1 _...T_ . The goal of RL is to learn a policy maximizing the sum of rewards during episodes, i.e., the return. In this setting, exploration poses many questions. If the environment provides no rewards, what should the agent look for? When should it act greedily with respect to the rewards it has found and stop looking for more? In the history of RL, many approaches have been proposed to tackle these questions. On one hand, classic single-environment approaches range from intrinsic motivation with visitation counts [2, 4, 13, 26, 53], optimism [1, 5, 25, 27, 31], or curiosity [7, 24, 35, 45, 49, 51], to bootstrapping [12, 33] or empowerment [29, 39]. On the other hand, we find approaches to incrementally learn tasks, such as transfer learning [58], continual learning [28], curriculum learning [32], and meta learning [38]. Below, we review approaches closely related to ours.
2While it can be argued that the real world has no explicit distinction between training and testing, we use this dichotomy only for the purpose of evaluation.

**Intrinsic motivation.** Exploration strategies relying on intrinsic rewards date back to Schmidhuber [47], who proposed to encourage exploration by visiting hard-to-predict states. More recently, the idea of auxiliary rewards to make up for the lack of external rewards has been extensively studied in RL, supported by evidence from psychology and neuroscience [20]. Several intrinsic rewards have been proposed, ranging from visitation count bonuses [4, 53] to bonuses based on prediction error of some quantity. For example, the agent may learn a dynamics model and try to predict the next state [24, 35, 48, 51]. By giving a bonus proportional to the prediction error, the agent is incentivized to explore unpredictable states. Schultheis et al. [49], instead, proposed to learn intrinsic rewards function by maximizing extrinsic rewards by meta-gradient. However, in these approaches exploration is agent-centric, i.e., based on an agent’s belief such as the forward model error. In contrast, with this work we propose additionally learning _environment-centric_ exploration policies. C-BET neither requires a model nor knowledge of extrinsic rewards. Instead, it encourages the agent to perform actions causing _interesting changes_ to the environment. We should note that while Raileanu and Rocktäschel [36] proposed a similar approach, their exploration policy lacks the transfer component and also requires to learn models.

**Transfer learning.** The idea of agents capable of incrementally learning tasks is well-known in the field of machine learning, with the first approaches dating back to the 90s’ [40, 41, 56]. In RL, recent methods have focused on policy and feature transfer. In the former, a pre-trained agent (teacher) is used to transfer behaviors to a new agent (student). Examples include policy distillation, where the student is trained to minimize the Kullback-Leibler divergence to the teacher [44] or to multiple teachers at the same time [55]. Alternative approaches, instead, directly reuse policies from source tasks to build the student policy [3, 17, 21]. In feature transfer, a pre-learned state representation is used to encourage exploration when tasks are presented to the agent [22, 59]. Similar to transfer RL, continual RL studies how learning on one or more tasks can help accelerate learning on different tasks, and how to prevent catastrophic forgetting [28, 42, 50]. Meta RL, instead, tries to exploit underlying common structures between tasks to learn new tasks more quickly [18, 38]. However, the setup in these approaches is not task-agnostic, i.e., task-specific policies are transferred rather than exploration policies. For example, after learning a policy maximizing the rewards of one task, the agent starts exploring guided by the same policy as a second task is given. Transfer is taskcentric rather than task-agnostic and environment-centric. Consequently, if tasks are too dissimilar information cannot be reused, even if the environments are similar. By contrast, in this work we propose learning task-agnostic exploration from one or many environments and show transfer to unseen environments. We should note that while Pathak et al. [35] did demonstrate fine-tuning on different maze maps, their focus and large-scale evaluations remain on tabula-rasa exploration.

## **3 Learning to Explore**

Our goal is to decouple the environment-centric nature of exploration from its agent-centric component. Contrary to prior work, we propose to first learn an environment-centric exploration policy and then to transfer it to unseen environments. The policy is driven by the inherent interestingness of states and is learned over time via interaction. First, during a pre-training phase, the agent interacts with many environments without any tasks and learns an exploration policy. Then, when new environments and tasks are presented, the agent uses the previously learned policy to explore more efficiently and learn task-specific policies. C-BET’s key components are (1) a novel intrinsic reward and the learning of a policy to disentangle exploration from exploitation, and (2) the use of such policy to help exploration for new tasks. Figures 2 and 3 summarize our framework.

Figure 4: **Visualization of intrinsic rewards (row) for the agent’s actions (column).** Brighter color denotes higher reward. Rewarding only state counts (top) does not provide useful feedback, and going to the corners is valued more than picking up the key. With the L2 norm of state changes (middle), the agent is biased in favor of moving, because its position is encoded with the highest value in the observation space. The resulting policy will prefer to navigate without picking up the key. By contrast, C-BET (bottom) gives picking up the key the highest reward.

We should note that Rajendran et al. [37] also proposed a transfer framework based on intrinsic rewards. In their work, the agent switches between practice episodes –where the agent receives only intrinsic rewards– and match episodes –giving only extrinsic rewards. However, practice episodes are simpler variations of match episodes (e.g., in Atari Pong the agent practices against itself) rather than different tasks as in C-BET. Furthermore, the intrinsic reward used in practice episodes is given by a function trained with meta-gradients to improve the extrinsic-reward return. That is, exploration is not task-agnostic as in C-BET, and extrinsic rewards are the main drive of the agent.

## **3.1 Interestingness of State-Action Pairs**

The natural world is filled with states or scenarios that are inherently interesting and our goal is to capture this inherent interestingness via intrinsic rewards. In this paper, we propose adding an environment-centric component of interestingness to the existing agent-centric component of surprise. Specifically, we hypothesize that the environment can _change_ on interaction, and the changes that are _rare_ are inherently interesting. That is, we penalize actions not affecting the environment, and favor actions producing rare changes. For instance, moving around, bumping into walls, or trying to open locked doors without keys all result in no change and thus will be of low interest.

We also want to keep the agent-centric component in exploration –that is, the exploration policy should look for surprises or unseen states. Thus, we further reward actions leading to less-visited states. By combining these two components, the resulting C-BET interest-based reward is

where _c_ ( _s, s[′]_ ) defines the _environment change_ of a transition ( _s, a, s[′]_ ), and _N_ denotes (pseudo)counts of changes and states. Figure 4 empirically shows its effectiveness. In Section 4 we discuss change encodings used in our experiments.

## **3.2 Exploration Learning**

In this phase, we want to learn task-agnostic exploration policies from interaction with many environments. The agent has no goal, but states where it can ‘die’ are still terminal. In this setting, we would like to treat the problem of learning exploration as an MDP with intrinsic-rewards only, and train the agent to maximize discounted intrinsic-returns averaged over episodes.

Formally, the agent explores many environments _E_ EXP = _{E_ 1 _, E_ 2 _, . . . , EN }_ , each governed by MDP _⟨Sn, A, P, ri, γi⟩_ . That is, each environment has its own states but the action space is the same, and all environments obey the same dynamics _P_ and the same intrinsic reward function _ri_ . The agent’s goal is to learn an exploration policy maximizing the sum of discounted intrinsic rewards, i.e., _π_ EXP( _s, a_ ) = arg max _π_ E _E,π_ [[�] _t[γ] i[t][r][i]_[(] _[s][t][, a][t]_[)]][.][To approximate the average, after a maximum number] of steps the environment is reset and a new episode starts, as typically done in RL. However, both common [7, 35, 36] and Eq. (1) intrinsic rewards decrease over time as the agent explores, to the point that they vanish to zero given enough samples. For instance, counts will grow to infinity, or prediction models error will go to zero. While this is not an issue in the tabula-rasa setup where the agent also gets extrinsic rewards, it can be problematic in the proposed task-agnostic exploration framework. Any policy, indeed, would be optimal if all rewards are zero.

4

To prevent Eq. (1) from vanishing, we randomly reset counts any given time step. To explain why resets need to be random, we start by considering ‘episodic counts’ proposed by Raileanu and Rocktäschel [36]. These counts are reset at the beginning of every episode to ensure that the agent does not go back and forth between a sequence of states with high rewards. While this work fine when extrinsic rewards are also given, it can be a problem if we learn only on intrinsic rewards. When counts are reset, the agent ‘forgets’ past trajectories and thinks that every state and change is new. If resets always happen at the end of an episode, then initial states will always get higher reward. Moreover, starting always with zero-counts may favor some trajectories and penalize others.

Figure 5: This chainworld illustrates that if counts are resets at the beginning of every episode, the learned policy will never visit D.

Consider for example the chainworld in Figure 5. The agent always starts in A, from where it can go to B or D. From B, it loops between B and C. From D, it cannot go anywhere else. The optimal exploration policy should visit all states uniformly, by randomly going to B and D. However, if we reset counts at every episode the agent forgets that it has already visited B and C. Thus, the intrinsic rewards for B and C are high again, and trajectory ABCBCBC... gives higher intrinsic return than ADDD... Consequently, the optimal policy with respect to episodic counts will always prefer to visit B rather than D.

The optimal exploration policy, instead, should have some randomness to visit the environment uniformly, while prioritizing interesting states. For this reason, we propose to reset counts at any given step with probability _p_ . When a new episode starts, counts may not be reset yet so the agent remembers what it has visited before. As the agent explores, on average common states and changes will have higher count more often, and the agent will correctly prefer rarer ones. In this paper, we propose _p ≤_ 1 _− γi_ where _γi_ is the intrinsic reward discount factor. This is a fitting choice because in an MDP the sum of discounted rewards can be interpreted as the expected sum of undiscounted rewards if every time step had a 1 _− γi_ probability of ending. Intuitively, this means that _γi_ implies a ‘life expectancy’ of 1 _/_ (1 _− γi_ ) steps, and thus resets should not happen more frequently than that.

The resulting MDP with Eq. (1) rewards and random count resets can be solved by any RL algorithm. However, we should note that this MDP is non-stationary, because the agent may receive different rewards for the same state, depending on how many times the state has been visited in the past. Nonetheless, classic intrinsic rewards –even in tabula-rasa exploration– either based on prediction errors [35, 36] or counts [4] also introduce non-stationarity because they change over time as well. In practice, this non-stationarity is not an issue because intrinsic rewards change slowly over time.

## **3.3 Exploration Transfer**

Now, the agent is presented with new environments and asked to solve tasks. Formally, each environment is governed by the standard MDP _⟨S, A, P, r, γ⟩_ and the agent’s goal is to learn a policy maximizing the sum of extrinsic rewards, i.e., _π_ TASK( _s, a_ ) = arg max _π_ E _π_ [[�] _t[γ][t][r]_[(] _[s][t][, a][t]_[)]][.][Note that] while during pre-training the policy was learned across all environments (one exploration policy for all environments), at transfer we learn one task-specific policy for each environment. In this phase, the interest-policy learned earlier drives exploration as tasks and environments are presented to the agent. In order not to forget interestingness over time, the exploration policy is added as a fixed bias to the task-specific policy, similarly to what Hailu and Sommer [21] proposed. Thanks to the decoupling of the interest-policy (based on the intrinsic reward) from the task-policy (based on the extrinsic reward), the latter can be also learned independently via any RL algorithm. In our experiments, we use IMPALA [16] to learn both _π_ EXP and _π_ TASK. IMPALA learns policies of the form _π_ ( _s, a_ ) = _σ_ ( _f_ ( _s, a_ )), where _σ_ is the softmax function. The policy is trained to maximize a function representing the value of states _V_ ( _s_ ), trained on the given rewards. In our framework, we combine the two policies as follows.
- During pre-training, by using intrinsic rewards we learn _Vi_ ( _s_ ) and _π_ EXP( _s, a_ ) = _σ_ ( _fi_ ( _s, a_ )).

- At transfer, we learn _Ve_ ( _s_ ) on extrinsic rewards. The policy is _π_ TASK( _s, a_ ) = _σ_ ( _fe_ ( _s, a_ ) + _fi_ ( _s, a_ )). The interestingness _fi_ is transferred but not trained, i.e., it acts as fixed bias to encourage interaction. Initially the policy follows _fi_ since _fe_ is initialized randomly. As it finds extrinsic rewards, the sum _fe_ + _fi_ becomes greedier with respect to extrinsic rewards, and _fe_ slowly overtakes _fi_[3] .

> 3 If exploration and the task goals are misaligned, we can decay exploration, e.g., _π_ TASK( _s, a_ ) = _σ_ ( _αfi_ ( _s, a_ )+ _fe_ ( _s, a_ )), where _α_ decays over time, similarly to common _ϵ_ -greedy policies.

Figure 6: **Examples of the environments used in our experiments.** In MiniGrid (left), the agent navigates through a grid and interacts with objects (keys, doors, boxes, and balls) to fulfill a task. In Habitat (right), the agent navigates through visually realistic rooms.

Note that we transfer only _fi_ (the policy) and not _Vi_ (the state value). We could think of transferring _Vi_ as fixed bias as well, i.e., by having _Ve_ ( _s_ ) = _V_ ( _s_ ) + _Vi_ ( _s_ ). The policy would be trained on _Ve_ –the states value with respect to the given task– where _Vi_ is fixed and only _V_ is updated. However, we believe it is more beneficial to isolate the exploratory component within the policy, in order to keep the task-specific value function targeted on extrinsic rewards. By not transferring _Vi_ , _Ve_ can be accurately trained on extrinsic rewards –that the agent will see often thanks to _fi_ from the pre-trained policy. _Ve_ , in turn, can make _π_ TASK greedy with respect to extrinsic rewards as _Ve_ is learned.

## **4 Experiments**

Our experiments are designed to highlight the benefits of disentangling the environment-centric nature of exploration from agent-centric behavior by learning a separate exploration policy and then transferring it to new environments. We stress that for learning task-agnostic exploration there are no standard benchmark environments, experimental setups, well-defined evaluation metrics, or even baselines to compare against. One of our contributions is to provide an exhaustive evaluation framework for the transfer exploration paradigm.

**Environments.** The experiments are divided into two main sections. The first is about MiniGrid [10] (Section 4.1), a set of procedurally-generated environments where the agent can interact with many objects. The second is about Habitat [46] (Section 4.2), a navigation simulator showcasing the generality of our MiniGrid experiments to a visually realistic domain.

**Change encoding.** In both MiniGrid and Habitat the agent partially observes the environment, since it cannot see through corners, closed door, or inside boxes, and has a limited field of view. Rather than egocentric views (i.e., what the agent sees in front of itself), we use 360 _[◦]_ panoramic views to count environment changes, as this is a rotation-invariant representation of the observed state. Similar to Chaplot et al. [8], we concatenate four egocentric views taken from 0 _[◦]_ , 90 _[◦]_ , 180 _[◦]_ , and 270 _[◦]_ with respect to the North. Then, the change of a transition is the difference between panoramic views pano( _s_ ), i.e., _c_ ( _s, s[′]_ ) := pano( _s[′]_ ) _−_ pano( _s_ ).
**Baselines.** We evaluate against the following algorithms. For more details, refer to Appendix A.1.

- _Count_ [4]. The intrinsic reward is inversely proportional to the next state visitation count.

- _Random Network Distillation (RND)_ [7]. The intrinsic reward is the prediction error of states’ random features between a trained network and a fixed one. This can be interpreted as similar to using state counts because the prediction improves states are seen more often.
- _Rewarding Impact-Driven Exploration (RIDE)_ [36]. The intrinsic reward is the prediction error between consecutive embedded states, normalized by episodic state counts.

- _Curiosity_ [35]. The intrinsic reward is the prediction error between consecutive states.

In Appendix B we investigate the importance of count resets, panoramic changes, and different count-based rewards. The source code is available at `https://github.com/sparisi/cbet/` .

## **4.1 MiniGrid Experiments**

MiniGrid environments [10] are procedurally-generated gridworlds where the agent can interact with objects, such as keys, doors, and boxes (Figure 6). Exploration is challenging because rewards are sparse, observations are partial, and specific actions are needed to visit all states (e.g., pickup key to open door). With MiniGrid, we can generate several pairs of train and test environments that are related but still different in many ways. These pairs enable evaluation of both the learning and transfer abilities of an exploration method and its ability to deal with unseen components.

**Implementation details.** All environments give a 7 _×_ 7 _×_ 3 partial observation encoding the content of the 7 _×_ 7 tiles in front of the agent (including the agent’s tile). The agent cannot see through walls, closed doors, or inside boxes. The action space is discrete: left, right, forward, pick up, drop, toggle, and done. For a complete description of the environments, we refer to Appendix A.4.

6

**Setups.** We present three setups, to study different exploration transfers against tabula-rasa.

- _MultiEnv (many-to-many transfer)._ The agent loops over three environments episode by episode, and learns the exploration policy using intrinsic rewards only. There is one state count and one change count for all three environments rather than separate counts for each. The environments are: KeyCorridorS3R3, BlockedUnlockPickup, and MultiRoom-N4-S5, and have been chosen for size and interaction variety: the first has both a locked and an unlocked door, a key, and a ball; the second adds a box; the third has more rooms. Note that even if these environments have all object types, the agent cannot experience all kinds of interactions. For example, it will not know that keys can be hidden in boxes, as in the ObstructedMazes. The policy is then transferred to ten environments, seven of which are new. A good intrinsic reward should help learn better exploration faster from multiple environments, thanks to sharing experience from diverse interaction.
- _SingleEnv (one-to-many transfer)._ The policy is pre-trained on a single environment. DoorKey and KeyCorridor are used for pre-training because they have some –but not all– objects.

- _Tabula-rasa (no pre-training / transfer)._ A task-specific policy is learned as in classic intrinsic motivation by summing intrinsic and extrinsic rewards. While it is a non-realistic setup, it is the most common RL exploration approach, and thus serves as baseline against our transfer framework.

**Evaluation metrics.** Our goal is to learn exploration policies that encourage interaction with the environments and transfer well to new environments, i.e., that can further be trained to solve extrinsic tasks faster. Therefore, we evaluate policies according to the following criteria.

- Unique interactions over 100 episodes at transfer to new environments, after intrinsic-reward pre-training (no extrinsic-reward training yet). Unique interactions are picks/drops/toggles resulting in new environment changes. For instance, repeatedly picking and dropping the same key in the same cell results in only two interactions.

- Tasks success rate over 100 episodes at transfer to new environments, after intrinsic-reward pretraining (no extrinsic-reward training yet). The task success rate denotes in how many episodes the exploration policy visits goal states –thus, would have already solved the environment task.

- Extrinsic return during extrinsic-reward training, after intrinsic-reward training.

## **4.1.1 MiniGrid Pre-Training Results**

Figure 7 shows results after pre-training in MultiEnv. In Appendix C we report results for the two SingleEnv setups as well. C-BET policy both interacts with the environment and find goal states more often than all baselines. As we will see in the next section, this will result in faster extrinsicreward learning. Furthermore, C-BET’s policy transfers well to all environments, even the ones with unknown dynamics (e.g., boxes in ObstructedMazes needs to be toggled to reveal keys). Of the baselines, only Count scores high average interactions and success rate, but it does not generalize as well as C-BET. Indeed, most of Count’s success comes from environments visited at pre-training (the first five), but most of its interactions are in environments with unseen dynamics (ObstructedMazes). That is, Count’s policy can explore familiar environments prioritizing state coverage (high success rate and few interactions), but not unfamiliar ones (low success rate yet high interactions).

Figure 7: **Unique interactions and success rate** at the beginning of transfer of policies pre-trained in MultiEnv. Not only C-BET interacts the most and achieves the highest success rate, but also interacts and succeeds in **all** environments. Naturally, it interacts more in environment with many keys/balls/boxes to pick (KeyCorridor, BlockedUnblockPickup, ObstructedMazes), and less if there is nothing to pick (MultiRooms). On the contrary, Count overfits to the training environments and performs well only on the first five. Other baselines perform poorly, almost as a random policy.

7

Figure 8: **MiniGrid task learning, for both transfer and tabula-rasa exploration.** The hardest tasks are outlined in red. C-BET (blue) from MultiEnv (top row under each environment) performs the best, starting with nearly optimal policies in most environments. This demonstrates the effectiveness of pre-training on multiple environments using the C-BET intrinsic reward.

Finally, RIDE, Curiosity, and RND baselines perform poorly. This is unsurprising if we consider that they rely on predictive models and that MiniGrid dynamics are deterministic and simple. Dynamics and embeddings models are learned quickly, without giving the policy time to explore. In Appendix C.3 we support this claim by showing the intrinsic reward trend during pre-training.

## **4.1.2 MiniGrid Transfer Results**

We transfer the exploration policies learned in Figure 7 as discussed in Section 3.3. Figure 8 shows how transfer setups (many-to-many and one-to-many) perform against tabula-rasa exploration. The first takeaway is that policies pre-trained with the C-BET intrinsic reward outperform baselines in both transfer and tabula-rasa. In MultiEnv transfer, C-BET performs the best, especially on the hardest environments (outlined in red). In particular, only C-BET is able to transfer to MultiRoom-N6. On the contary, Count –that can solve it in tabula-rasa– fails at transfer. C-BET is also the only solving ObstructedMaze-2Dlhb –the hardest environment among the ten– even in tabula-rasa. The second takeaway is that baselines relying on models are not suited to the transfer framework. RIDE, Curiosity and RND perform better in the tabula-rasa setup (last row), except for the easiest environments (Unlock and DoorKey), meaning that transfer is actually harmful. These results are in line with Figure 7, where only C-BET and Count show success at offline transfer. Furthermore, RIDE, Curiosity and RND perform worst when transfer is from MultiEnv, highlighting that their intrinsic rewards are not suited for a multi-environment setup.

Finally, no algorithm learns MultiRoom-N12-10, not even C-BET despite showing some success in Figure 7. This is due to the randomly-initialized _fe_ of the task-specific policy, hindering the pre-trained exploration policy success.

Figure 11: **Scene coverage** of exploration policies during pre-training (2M steps) in Apartment 0. Darker red cells denote higher visitation rates. Only C-BET visits all of the scene uniformly.

## **4.2 Habitat Experiments**

To demonstrate that C-BET’s efficacy extends to realistic settings with visual inputs, we perform experiments on Habitat [46] with Replica scenes [52].

**Implementation details.** Egocentric views have resolution 64 _×_ 64 _×_ 3. The action space is discrete: forward 0.25 meter, turn 10 _[◦]_ left, and turn 10 _[◦]_ right. To ease computational demands, we use #Exploration [54] with static hashing to map both egocentric and panoramic views to hash codes and count their occurrences with a hash table. More details in Appendix A.6.

**Setups.** We evaluate Habitat on the _one-to-many transfer_ . First, we pre-train exploration policies with only intrinsic rewards in one scene. Then, we evaluate them on new scenes without further learning. Given a fixed amount of steps, better policies will visit more of the new scenes.

**Evaluation metrics.** Unlike MiniGrid, we use no extrinsic rewards in Habitat. Since the agent has to navigate through rooms and spaces, we evaluate exploration policies using scene coverage measured by the agent’s true state in Cartesian coordinates (not accessible by the agent)[4] . Faster, larger and more uniform coverage corresponds to better exploration. Plots show mean and confidence interval over seven random seeds per method with no smoothing.

## **4.2.1 Habitat Pre-Training Results**

We pre-train exploration policies on Apartment 0 (Figure 6), one of the largest Replica scene in the dataset. Figures 9 and 11 show state coverage throughout and at the end of pre-training, respectively. C-BET explores more efficiently, covering twice as much of the scene than all baselines. In particular, at the end of pre-training it has explored almost all Apartment 0 uniformly. In Appendix E.1 we also report C-BET results when environment changes are encoded with egocentric views rather than panoramic views.

## **4.2.2 Habitat Transfer Results**

Here, we evaluate scene coverage of pre-trained policies in seven unseen scenes for episodes of fixed steps. A better exploration policy will exhibit generalization by covering a larger portion of all scenes as evenly as possible, an impressive feat given the visual complexity of the observations. Indeed, generalization is harder than MiniGrid because the lighting, colors, objects, and layout can be very different between scenes (see Figure 14 in the Appendix). Figures 10 and 12 show that, once again, C-BET clearly outperforms all baselines. Its exploration policy transfer well to all scenes, as it uniformly discovers more states. No baseline comes closer to its results. Actually, in many scenes baselines perform worse than a random policy.

Figure 12: **Scene coverage** of exploration policies after 100 episodes (50,000 total steps) at offline transfer to Room 0. C-BET outperforms baselines and exhibits great transfer by visiting all of the scene uniformly. In Appendix E.2, we show heatmaps for all transfer scenes.

## **5 Discussion**

In this paper, we proposed a paradigm change in task-agnostic exploration. Instead of studying task-agnostic exploration in isolated environments, we proposed to (1) learn task-agnostic exploration policies from one or multiple environments, and (2) transfer learned exploration policies to unseen environments at testing time. In our setup, the agent interacts with the environment without any extrinsic goal and learns to explore environments in a task-agnostic manner. To this end, we proposed a novel intrinsic reward to encourage interaction with the environment and the visitation of unseen states. Subsequently, our agent effectively transfers its exploration policy to unseen environments.

**Advantages.** The proposed two-phase framework achieves two important features, making it fundamentally different from prior work. First, we account for _environment interestingness_ without relying on additional models. Instead, we use a data-driven approach, estimating the rarity of states and environment changes. Rare changes are considered more interesting, actions causing them receive higher intrinsic rewards, and the agent is encouraged to perform them again. For instance, when navigating through rooms, opening doors will be more interesting due to rarity: the agent must navigate to the corresponding key, collect it, navigate to the door, and finally open it. Thus opening a door is rarer than picking up a key, in turn rarer than simple navigation movements. Furthermore, relying on environment-centric intrinsic rewards rather than task-centric extrinsic rewards facilitates learning from multiple environments at the same time.

Second, contrary to prior transfer and continual learning algorithms we transfer policies learned on _interestingness of the environment_ rather than task-specific policies. In the interest-based pre-training phase, we learn through interaction with the environment in a task-agnostic fashion, i.e., the agent freely explores the environment without any extrinsic task.

**Limitations.** In this paper, we assumed that interacting with the environment while looking for rare changes helps find better extrinsic rewards faster. However, exploration and the task goals may be misaligned, thus a highly exploratory policy may slow down the discovery of extrinsic rewards. For instance, the environment may have dangerous states or harmful objects that the agent should avoid, even though they would make it curious during pre-training. Furthermore, C-BET is currently tied to (pseudo)counts to compute the rarity of states and changes. While extensions to continuous spaces exist, count-based metrics are more suited for discrete spaces.

**Impact.** RL can positively impact real-world problems, e.g., healthcare [19], assistive robotics [15], and climate change [43]. Yet, RL may have negative impacts, e.g., in autonomous weapons or workforce displacement [6]. Our work focuses on exploration in RL. Better understanding of what is interesting to do or visit helps exploration in unseen environments, as the agent will not waste time with random actions. Similarly, transferring policies learned in a related setting –as we do– can help narrow the range of the agent’s expected behavior. Conversely, in many real-world scenarios exploration by curiosity and interestingness is unacceptable. For instance, autonomous cars cannot run over pedestrians just for the sake of curiosity. At present, our work is far from these impacts, but we hope to direct research to focus more on learning from multiple environments and transferring experiences, while at the same time ensuring the safety and reliability of autonomous agents.

The authors would like to thank Davide Tateo for his thoughtful discussions, Roberta Raileanu for providing support for RIDE’s codebase, and Sudeep Dasari for helping run experiments. VD and DP were supported in part by NSF Fellowship and DARPA Machine Common Sense grant, respectively.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes Change-Based Exploration Transfer (C-BET), a method to learn task-agnostic exploration policies by combining agent-centric curiosity with an environment-centric 'interestingness' metric based on rare state changes. The authors evaluate this approach in MiniGrid and Habitat environments, demonstrating that pre-training on multiple environments and transferring the exploration policy leads to faster acquisition of task-specific skills compared to tabula-rasa baselines.

The paper addresses a significant challenge in reinforcement learning by framing exploration as a lifelong, transferable process rather than a one-off task. The proposed dual-component reward structure is intuitive and shows strong empirical performance in the selected benchmarks. However, the reliance on pseudo-counts and the lack of a formal treatment of environment-specific noise or non-stationarity limit the paper's theoretical robustness.

Overview issues:

- Sensitivity to Stochastic Transitions and Noisy Environments: The intrinsic reward function in Equation (1) relies on counts of state changes, which are highly susceptible to the 'noisy TV' problem. In environments with stochastic dynamics or high-frequency visual noise, the agent will perceive these transitions as 'interesting' because they are rare, leading to degenerate behavior where the agent fixates on stochastic elements. The paper lacks a mechanism to distinguish between state changes caused by meaningful interactions (e.g., picking up a key) and those caused by environmental noise. Without a filtering mechanism or a more robust representation of 'interestingness' beyond raw count-based rarity, the method's claim of being task-agnostic is weakened by its potential to overfit to stochasticity.

- Ambiguity in the Definition of 'Environment Change': Section 3.1 and Section 4 define environment change as the difference between panoramic views, c(s,s') := pano(s') - pano(s). This definition is problematic in high-dimensional or visually complex environments where small changes in lighting or viewpoint can result in large differences in the pixel-level panoramic representation. The paper does not provide an ablation or a theoretical justification for why this specific difference metric is invariant to the 'interestingness' of the objects themselves versus the geometry of the scene. If the metric is not invariant to irrelevant visual changes, the agent may be incentivized to explore areas with high visual variance rather than areas with high interaction potential.

- Lack of Theoretical Guarantees for Policy Transfer: The framework assumes that a policy learned via intrinsic rewards in a source environment will generalize to a target environment. However, the paper does not address the conditions under which this transfer is valid, such as the required similarity in transition dynamics or state-space structure between the source and target environments. Given that the exploration policy acts as a fixed bias in the transfer phase (Section 3.3), there is a risk of negative transfer if the 'interesting' objects in the source environment are irrelevant or misleading in the target environment. A theoretical discussion or a more rigorous empirical analysis of the failure modes of this transfer mechanism would strengthen the paper's claims.

- Incomplete Evaluation of Non-Stationarity: The authors introduce random resets of counts with probability p to mitigate the vanishing reward problem (Section 3.2). While this is a practical heuristic, it introduces a non-stationary component to the MDP that is not fully analyzed. The paper claims this is not an issue because rewards change slowly, but it does not account for the impact of this non-stationarity on the convergence of the underlying RL algorithm (IMPALA). A more detailed empirical analysis showing the stability of the intrinsic value function V_i under this reset policy would be necessary to ensure the learned exploration policy is actually optimal.

- Absence of a formal failure case for negative transfer: The paper claims that C-BET learns a general exploration policy, but it does not demonstrate where this transfer fails. Given that the exploration policy is added as a fixed bias, it is plausible that 'interesting' objects in a source environment (e.g., a doorbell) could actively distract an agent in a target environment where that object is a distractor or irrelevant. To validate the robustness of the transfer, the authors should construct a 'negative transfer' experiment where the pre-training environment contains objects that are explicitly irrelevant or harmful to the goal in the test environment. Showing how the agent overcomes or fails to overcome this bias would provide necessary evidence of the method's limitations and the boundaries of its 'task-agnostic' claim.

- Lack of sensitivity analysis for the reset probability p: The random reset mechanism for counts (Section 3.2) is a central component of the C-BET framework, yet its sensitivity is not analyzed. The choice of 'p' is claimed to relate to the discount factor, but the paper provides no empirical evidence of how performance varies as 'p' deviates from this heuristic. A brief ablation study showing the performance of C-BET across a range of 'p' values (e.g., 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2) in the MiniGrid suite would clarify whether the method is stable or if it requires fine-tuning for each domain, which would contradict the 'task-agnostic' motivation.

- Missing comparison to a non-transfer baseline with equal compute: The paper compares C-BET against tabula-rasa baselines that do not have the benefit of pre-training. While C-BET performs better, it is unclear if the gains are due to the 'environment-centric' insight or simply the result of having more experience (the pre-training phase). To isolate the contribution of the C-BET architecture, the authors should include a baseline where a standard agent (e.g., RND or Count) is pre-trained on the same multi-environment set for the same number of steps and then transferred. This would demonstrate whether C-BET's specific formulation of interestingness is superior to simply giving standard agents more training time across multiple environments.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add a controlled experiment using an environment with stochastic transitions (e.g., a 'noisy TV' variant) to demonstrate the robustness of the C-BET reward function compared to standard curiosity baselines.

- Provide a more formal justification or analysis of the panoramic change encoding, specifically addressing how it handles visual variance that is not related to object interaction.

- Include an ablation study or discussion on the sensitivity of the transfer performance to the dissimilarity between source and target environments, clearly identifying when the learned exploration policy fails.

- Clarify the stability of the training process under the proposed random count reset mechanism, perhaps by reporting the variance of the intrinsic value function during pre-training.

comments.title: Mathematical flaw in L2 norm explanation
comments.quote: With the L2 norm of state changes (middle), the agent is biased in favor of moving, because its position is encoded with the highest value in the observation space. The resulting policy will prefer to navigate without picking up the key.
comments.explanation: The explanation for why the L2 norm fails is mathematically incorrect. The L2 norm of the difference between two state vectors is determined by the magnitude of the change in the vector components, not by the absolute magnitude of the values within the state vector itself. If the agent moves, the change in the position index contributes to the norm regardless of whether the position is encoded as 10 or 0.1. The bias toward movement is a consequence of the agent's position changing at every timestep, which ensures a non-zero difference vector, rather than the scale of the encoding. Please rewrite this to explain that the L2 norm is sensitive to any state change, including trivial movement, which dominates the reward signal compared to the sparse event of picking up the key.

comments.title: Dimensional inconsistency in intrinsic reward formula
comments.quote: r _ {i} (s, a, s ^ {\prime}) = 1 / \left(N \left(s ^ {\prime}\right) + N (c)\right), \tag {1}
comments.explanation: The terms N(s') and N(c) operate on different cardinalities. In reinforcement learning, state-space size and change-space size are often orders of magnitude apart, meaning the sum in the denominator is dominated by the larger set, effectively nullifying the contribution of the smaller set. Without a learned or hyperparameter-based weighting factor, the agent cannot reliably balance environment-centric and agent-centric exploration. Please rewrite the reward as r_i(s, a, s') = 1 / (w_1 * N(s') + w_2 * N(c)) where w_1 and w_2 are weighting parameters to normalize the contributions of the two components.

comments.title: Conceptual flaw in reset probability justification
comments.quote: In this paper, we propose  $p \leq 1 - \gamma_i$  where  $\gamma_i$  is the intrinsic reward discount factor. This is a fitting choice because in an MDP the sum of discounted rewards can be interpreted as the expected sum of undiscounted rewards if every time step had a  $1 - \gamma_i$  probability of ending.
comments.explanation: The justification for the reset probability p is conceptually flawed. The reset mechanism described does not terminate the episode; it only resets the count-based intrinsic reward signal. In a standard MDP, termination at time t implies the agent receives no further rewards. Here, the agent continues to receive rewards after a count reset, just with a modified intrinsic reward value. The 'life expectancy' argument is therefore invalid because the agent's trajectory is not truncated by the reset. Please remove the claim that p <= 1 - gamma_i is a 'fitting choice' based on termination probability, or provide a derivation showing the equivalence of the expected returns under the reset process and the discounted MDP.

comments.title: Ambiguity in panoramic difference scalarization
comments.quote: Then, the change of a transition is the difference between panoramic views  $\mathsf{pano}(s)$ , i.e.,  $c(s,s') := \mathsf{pano}(s') - \mathsf{pano}(s)$ .
comments.explanation: The definition provided for c(s,s') is ambiguous because it implies a vector subtraction of two panoramic views. If pano(s) is a high-dimensional representation, the difference is a vector, not a scalar. However, the intrinsic reward function requires a scalar value to represent rarity. If you intend to use the norm of this difference, such as ||pano(s') - pano(s)||_2, this must be explicitly stated. Without a norm or a projection to a scalar, the expression c(s,s') cannot be used directly in the count-based reward mechanisms.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of count-based exploration
comments.quote: For example, if the agent uses a visitation count model and has seen many objects of one kind in one environment, it would not explore the same type of objects again in a new environment.
comments.explanation: The claim that count-based models inherently fail to explore objects seen in previous environments is inaccurate. Modern count-based methods often operate on state representations where counts are local to the current episode or environment instance, or they use pseudo-counts that adapt. The limitation described is a design choice of specific implementations rather than a fundamental property of count-based exploration. Please rephrase to clarify that this is a limitation of naive or global count-based implementations rather than the entire class of count-based methods.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'task-agnostic' definition
comments.quote: Common approaches for task-agnostic exploration learn tabula-rasa –the agent assumes isolated environments and no prior knowledge or experience.
comments.explanation: The authors define 'task-agnostic' exploration in a way that conflates 'learning without extrinsic rewards' with 'learning without prior experience.' In standard reinforcement learning, task-agnostic exploration refers to methods that do not rely on a specific reward function, not necessarily methods that must start from scratch. By framing the lack of prior experience as the defining feature of existing 'task-agnostic' approaches, the authors create a false dichotomy. It would be more precise to distinguish between 'single-task' and 'multi-task' or 'lifelong' exploration paradigms rather than redefining 'task-agnostic' to exclude pre-training.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a two-phase framework for task-agnostic exploration that pre-trains an exploration policy across multiple environments and transfers it to new tasks. The core contribution is a combined intrinsic reward using state and change counts, paired with a fixed-bias policy transfer mechanism. While the multi-environment transfer setup addresses a real gap in the literature, the implementation relies on architectural specifics and privileged observations that limit generalizability.

The paper tackles a meaningful gap in reinforcement learning by shifting focus from isolated, tabula-rasa exploration to multi-environment transfer. The proposed C-BET method demonstrates strong empirical performance in structured gridworlds and simulated navigation. However, the theoretical grounding of the intrinsic reward and the narrow transfer mechanism require clarification before the claims can be fully accepted.

Overview issues:

- The 'environment-centric' component is trajectory-dependent rather than intrinsic to the environment: Section 3.1 defines environment-centric interestingness via $N(c)$, the count of state changes $c(s,s')$. Change counts are inherently tied to the agent's trajectory and action space. An action that produces a 'rare' change under one policy may be common under another or in a different environment. This contradicts the paper's framing that some objects demand curiosity independent of the agent's beliefs. The metric essentially measures how often the agent has caused a transition, not an inherent property of the state or object. To strengthen this claim, the authors should compute change statistics over random or uniformly distributed policies to demonstrate that the 'interestingness' signal persists independently of the learned exploration policy.

- The transfer mechanism is tightly coupled to IMPALA's architecture: Section 3.3 describes transferring the exploration policy by adding the pre-trained policy logits $f_i(s,a)$ as a fixed bias to the task-specific policy logits $f_e(s,a)$. This approach is specific to IMPALA's shared feature extractor and separate policy heads. It does not generalize to other policy parameterizations, such as separate networks for exploration and task, or model-based architectures. Consequently, the 'transfer' is more of a weighted action selection during task training rather than a reusable exploration policy. The paper should either generalize the transfer mechanism to be architecture-agnostic or explicitly frame the contribution as a policy combination technique for IMPALA-like architectures.

- Random count resets induce severe non-stationarity that complicates policy optimization: Section 3.2 introduces random resets of state and change counts with probability $p$ to prevent intrinsic rewards from vanishing. While the authors acknowledge the resulting MDP is non-stationary, they dismiss the issue by noting that 'intrinsic rewards change slowly over time.' Random resets, however, cause abrupt, stochastic changes to the reward function at every time step. Standard on-policy algorithms like IMPALA assume a stationary reward distribution during an episode, and such abrupt changes can lead to high variance in policy gradients and unstable learning. The paper lacks analysis of how the reset probability $p$ interacts with the discount factor $	heta_i$ to affect policy convergence. A sensitivity analysis or theoretical bound on the expected reward drift would be necessary to validate the stability of this setup.

- The change metric assumes full 360-degree visibility, contradicting the partial-observation setting: Section 4 states that environments are partially observed, yet the change encoding $c(s,s')$ relies on concatenating four egocentric views to form a rotation-invariant 360-degree panorama. This assumes the agent has access to a full environmental snapshot at each step, which is neither available in standard partial-observation benchmarks nor realistic for physical agents with limited sensors. Using panoramic views effectively turns the problem into a fully observed one, inflating the agent's capability to detect 'rare changes.' The authors should evaluate the method under strict partial observability, using only the agent's actual egocentric observations to compute change counts, to demonstrate that the approach works without privileged sensory information.

- Baseline evaluations are skewed toward deterministic, discrete environments: The MiniGrid experiments operate in deterministic environments where prediction-error baselines naturally fail because their models converge instantly. The paper correctly notes this but frames it as a general advantage of count-based methods. This setup does not test the proposed method's ability to handle stochastic dynamics, continuous state spaces, or high-dimensional observations where count-based bonuses typically suffer from the curse of dimensionality. The Habitat experiments use static hashing, which mitigates the curse of dimensionality but introduces its own discretization artifacts. The paper should include a discussion or experiment on how the method performs in stochastic or continuous domains, where the core assumption of counting rare transitions may break down.

- Missing ablation of agent-centric versus environment-centric reward terms: Equation 1 combines state visitation counts and change counts into a single intrinsic reward, yet the paper never isolates these components. Without an ablation, readers cannot verify whether the claimed synergy between surprise-seeking and interestingness detection actually drives performance or if one term dominates. The authors should train C-BET variants using only $1/N(s')$ or only $1/N(c)$ on the MultiEnv setup and report the unique interactions and success rate metrics. This comparison is standard for intrinsic reward papers and directly tests the necessity of the joint formulation.

- Lack of comparison to continual learning and meta-RL baselines: The introduction frames the work as a shift from tabula-rasa exploration to multi-environment transfer, yet the experiments only compare against classic intrinsic motivation methods and a tabula-rasa baseline. Established continual learning and meta-RL approaches already address how to retain and reuse knowledge across environments. To substantiate the paradigm change claim, the authors must benchmark against at least one continual learning method or a meta-RL baseline. This comparison would clarify whether the proposed fixed-bias policy transfer offers a distinct advantage over existing transfer-learning frameworks.

- Missing sensitivity analysis for the random count reset probability: Section 3.2 introduces a random count reset probability $p$ to prevent intrinsic rewards from vanishing, but the paper provides no empirical analysis of how different values of $p$ affect learning stability or policy coverage. The authors only state that $p \le 1 - \gamma_i$ is a fitting choice based on discount factor intuition. Readers will wonder how sensitive the method is to this hyperparameter across different environment complexities. The authors should include a sensitivity analysis varying $p$ across the MiniGrid and Habitat setups and report the resulting training curves and final exploration metrics. This validation is necessary to confirm that the proposed reset mechanism reliably prevents reward vanishing without destabilizing the policy optimization.

Recommendation: Major revision. The paper addresses an important shift in exploration research, but the current formulation of the intrinsic reward, the architectural constraints of the transfer mechanism, and the reliance on privileged observations limit the generalizability of the claims. Addressing the non-stationarity analysis and broadening the evaluation scope will strengthen the manuscript significantly.

Revision targets:

- Provide a sensitivity analysis or theoretical discussion on how random count resets affect policy gradient variance and convergence stability in the non-stationary MDP.

- Generalize the transfer mechanism beyond IMPALA's fixed-bias logits, or explicitly limit the claims to architectures that support additive policy biases.

- Re-evaluate the change-based reward under strict partial observability using only egocentric views to confirm that the method does not rely on privileged 360-degree sensory input.

- Include experiments or a detailed discussion on how the count-based intrinsic reward scales to stochastic dynamics or continuous state spaces where the curse of dimensionality applies.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of empowerment as agent-centric
comments.quote: Most existing task-agnostic exploration approaches, such as visitation counts, curiosity, or empowerment, define intrinsic rewards in an agent-centric manner: they encourage exploration of unseen parts of the environment based on the agent's own belief.
comments.explanation: This categorization is incorrect. Empowerment measures the mutual information between actions and future states, capturing control over transition dynamics. It does not rely on the agent's visitation history or belief state, nor does it specifically target 'unseen parts.' Unlike curiosity or count-based methods, empowerment is a property of the environment's dynamics. The authors should remove empowerment from the list of agent-centric methods or clarify that they are referring specifically to belief-based curiosity variants.

comments.title: Contradictory characterization of transfer learning methods
comments.quote:
In feature transfer, a pre-learned state representation is used to encourage exploration when tasks are presented to the agent [22, 59]. Similar to transfer RL, continual RL studies how learning on one or more tasks can help accelerate learning on different tasks, and how to prevent catastrophic forgetting [28, 42, 50]. Meta RL, instead, tries to exploit underlying common structures between tasks to learn new tasks more quickly [18, 38].

However, the setup in these approaches is not task-agnostic, i.e., task-specific policies are transferred rather than exploration policies.
comments.explanation: The preceding sentences explicitly describe representation-based transfer, continual RL, and meta-RL as leveraging features, optimization landscapes, or initialization strategies. The subsequent blanket claim that these methods transfer 'task-specific policies' directly contradicts the definitions just provided. The authors likely intend to contrast their approach with policy-distillation methods, but the current phrasing invalidates their own descriptions. Rewrite the claim to specify that many of these approaches remain task-centric, as they often rely on transferring task-specific policies or fixed behavioral priors rather than learning general exploration policies.

comments.title: Inconsistent formulation of combined intrinsic reward
comments.quote:
By combining these two components, the resulting C-BET interest-based reward is

$$
r_i(s, a, s') = 1 / (N(s') + N(c)), \tag{1}
$$
comments.explanation: The prose describes 'adding' an environment-centric component to an agent-centric component, which conventionally implies an additive reward structure (e.g., $r_i = 1/N(s') + 1/N(c)$). Equation (1), however, places both counts in a shared denominator. This algebraic choice significantly alters the reward's scaling and sensitivity: under Eq. (1), a frequently visited state ($N(s') \gg 1$) suppresses the reward regardless of how rare the change $c$ is, whereas an additive formulation would still grant a baseline bonus for rare changes. If the shared-denominator structure is intentional, the text should clarify that the components are combined via a harmonic or multiplicative mechanism rather than 'added.' Otherwise, rewrite Eq. (1) to match the stated additive combination.

comments.title: Type mismatch in policy optimization objective
comments.quote: The agent's goal is to learn an exploration policy maximizing the sum of discounted intrinsic rewards, i.e.,  $\pi_{\mathrm{exp}}(s,a) = \arg \max_{\pi}\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{E},\pi}[\sum_t\gamma_i^t r_i(s_t,a_t)]$ .
comments.explanation: The left-hand side $\pi_{\mathrm{exp}}(s,a)$ denotes a policy's action probability at a specific state, which is a scalar value. The right-hand side $\arg \max_{\pi} \mathbb{E}[\dots]$ denotes the optimal policy itself, which is a function mapping states to action distributions. Equating a scalar to a function creates a type mismatch. Rewrite the equation as $\pi_{\mathrm{exp}} = \arg \max_{\pi} \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{E},\pi}[\sum_t\gamma_i^t r_i(s_t,a_t)]$ to correctly define the optimal exploration policy as a function.

comments.title: Unexplained relationship between value and policy networks
comments.quote: At transfer, we learn  $V_{e}(s)$  on extrinsic rewards. The policy is  $\pi_{\mathrm{TASK}}(s,a) = \sigma (f_e(s,a) + f_i(s,a))$ . The interestingness  $f_{i}$  is transferred but not trained, i.e., it acts as fixed bias to encourage interaction.
comments.explanation: The text states that a state-value function $V_e(s)$ is learned on extrinsic rewards, but immediately defines the task policy using a logit function $f_e(s,a)$ without explaining how $f_e$ is obtained. In actor-critic architectures like IMPALA, the value network $V(s)$ and policy network (logits $f(s,a)$) are distinct components trained with different objectives. The authors should clarify whether $f_e$ is a newly initialized policy network trained on extrinsic rewards, or if it is derived from $V_e$. Add a sentence clarifying that $f_e$ is a separate policy network trained on extrinsic rewards after the policy definition to address the missing architectural link.

comments.title: Incorrect description of policy training objective
comments.quote: The policy is trained to maximize a function representing the value of states  $V(s)$ , trained on the given rewards. In our framework, we combine the two policies as follows.
comments.explanation: This sentence conflates the training objectives of the policy and the value function. Policies are trained to maximize expected cumulative reward, not to maximize a value function. Value functions are trained to approximate expected returns. In actor-critic methods, the value function provides baseline or advantage estimates to stabilize policy updates, but the policy itself is not trained to 'maximize $V(s)$'. Rewrite the sentence to state that the value function $V(s)$ is trained to estimate expected returns, and the policy is updated via gradient ascent on the expected return.

comments.title: Inaccurate description of RIDE's intrinsic reward
comments.quote: Rewarding Impact-Driven Exploration (RIDE) [36]. The intrinsic reward is the prediction error between consecutive embedded states, normalized by episodic state counts.
comments.explanation: This description conflates RIDE with count-based exploration methods. The original RIDE formulation computes the prediction error of the state transition impact but does not divide or normalize this error by episodic visitation counts. Normalization by state counts is a hallmark of count-based bonuses, not impact-driven curiosity. This inaccuracy misrepresents a key baseline and may confuse readers comparing the methods. Rewrite the description to state that the intrinsic reward is the prediction error of the impact between consecutive embedded states.

comments.title: Contradiction in SingleEnv pre-training setup description
comments.quote: SingleEnv (one-to-many transfer). The policy is pre-trained on a single environment. DoorKey and KeyCorridor are used for pre-training because they have some –but not all– objects.
comments.explanation: The section heading and opening sentence state that the policy is pre-trained on 'a single environment,' but the very next sentence lists two environments ('DoorKey and KeyCorridor') used for pre-training. This creates a direct contradiction regarding the number of environments involved in the SingleEnv setup. Clarify whether SingleEnv refers to separate experimental runs for each of the two environments, or if the pre-training actually combines them (which would contradict the 'single environment' claim).

comments.title: RND misclassified as a model-based baseline
comments.quote: The second takeaway is that baselines relying on models are not suited to the transfer framework. RIDE, Curiosity and RND perform better in the tabula-rasa setup (last row), excep
comments.explanation: RND (Random Network Distillation) does not learn an environmental model; it trains a predictor to match a fixed random target network. Grouping it with RIDE and Curiosity under 'baselines relying on models' is technically inaccurate. The sentence should specify 'prediction-error or model-based baselines' or explicitly separate RND from the model-based methods to avoid mischaracterizing its architecture.

comments.title: Logical inconsistency in transfer failure explanation
comments.quote: Finally, no algorithm learns MultiRoom-N12-10, not even C-BET despite showing some success in Figure 7. This is due to the randomly-initialized  $f_{e}$  of the task-specific policy, hindering the pre-trained exploration policy success.
comments.explanation: The transfer mechanism described in Section 3.3 adds the pre-trained exploration policy logits as a fixed bias to the task policy. If the exploration policy is fixed during task training, its performance cannot be hindered by the random initialization of $f_e$. The failure to solve MultiRoom-N12-10 likely stems from the task policy $f_e$ struggling to learn the task alongside the exploration bias, or from instability in the combined policy. Clarify the training dynamics of $f_e$ and $f_i$ during transfer, and correct the causal claim regarding which policy is hindered.

comments.title: Ambiguity in hash table usage for change counts
comments.quote: To ease computational demands, we use #Exploration [54] with static hashing to map both egocentric and panoramic views to hash codes and count their occurrences with a hash table.
comments.explanation: The implementation description states that the hash table counts view occurrences, but the proposed method relies on change counts $N(c)$ to measure environment-centric interestingness. Counting static occurrences of egocentric or panoramic views does not directly yield change counts, as changes depend on transitions between consecutive states. Clarify how the hash table is used to compute change counts, since this distinction is central to the algorithm's novelty and performance. Without this clarification, it is unclear whether the reported results reflect the proposed change-based metric or a simpler visitation-count baseline. Add a brief explanation of how change counts are derived from the hash table immediately after this sentence.

comments.title: Misleading cross-reference to MiniGrid results
comments.quote: Figures 10 and 12 show that, once again, C-BET clearly outperforms all baselines. Its exploration policy transfer well to all scenes, as it uniformly discovers more states.
comments.explanation: Figure 10 displays results from the MiniGrid pre-training experiments in Section 4.1.1, not the Habitat transfer evaluation discussed here. Citing it alongside Figure 12 conflates two entirely different benchmarks and confuses the reader about which environment is being analyzed. Remove the reference to Figure 10 from this paragraph. Attribute the Habitat performance claims exclusively to Figure 12 to maintain clear separation between the MiniGrid and Habitat results.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim on multi-environment learning advantage
comments.quote: Rare changes are considered more interesting, actions causing them receive higher intrinsic rewards, and the agent is encouraged to perform them again. For instance, when navigating through rooms, opening doors will be more interesting due to rarity: the agent must navigate to the corresponding key, collect it, navigate to the door, and finally open it. Thus opening a door is rarer than picking up a key, in turn rarer than simple navigation movements. Furthermore, relying on environment-centric intrinsic rewards rather than task-centric extrinsic rewards facilitates learning from multiple environments at the same time.
comments.explanation: This assertion posits a fundamental advantage of environment-centric rewards for simultaneous multi-environment training, yet the discussion provides no theoretical justification or empirical evidence to support it. The paper's experiments evaluate transfer to unseen environments but do not isolate the specific effect of using environment-centric versus task-conditioned rewards during joint training. Without an ablation or citation demonstrating that task-centric rewards inherently fail or underperform in this setting, the claim remains speculative. Readers will question why decoupling from task objectives is necessary for multi-environment learning when task-conditioned intrinsic motivation is a standard and effective baseline. Rewrite the claim to frame it as a practical design benefit rather than an unverified theoretical advantage.

comments.title: Overstated distinction from continual learning methods
comments.quote: Second, contrary to prior transfer and continual learning algorithms we transfer policies learned on interestingness of the environment rather than task-specific policies. In the interest-based pre-training phase, we learn through interaction with the environment in a task-agnostic fashion, i.e., the agent freely explores the environment without any extrinsic task.
comments.explanation: The text positions the proposed method as fundamentally distinct from continual learning and transfer algorithms, but the distinction relies solely on the training objective rather than the transfer mechanism itself. Many continual learning frameworks also train on auxiliary or general tasks to learn transferable representations, and the paper's fixed-bias policy combination does not inherently break from standard continual learning pipelines. Without clarifying how the architectural or algorithmic transfer strategy differs from replay buffers, regularization, or meta-learning approaches, this claim reads as a distinction without a clear difference. The authors should explicitly map their transfer mechanism against established continual learning techniques to justify the 'contrary' framing. Add a clarifying sentence after the quote such as 'Unlike continual learning methods that rely on replay buffers or parameter regularization to prevent forgetting, our approach leverages a fixed-bias combination of exploration and task policies to enable zero-shot transfer.' to ground the claim in the paper's specific methodology.

comments.title: Intrinsic reward mechanism lacks formal definition
comments.quote: Instead, we use a data-driven approach, estimating the rarity of states and environment changes. Rare changes are considered more interesting, actions causing them receive higher intrinsic rewards, and the agent is encouraged to perform them again.
comments.explanation: The formal results section defines the policy combination $\pi_{\mathrm{TASK}}(s,a) = \sigma (f_e(s,a) + f_i(s,a))$ and outlines the two-phase training procedure, but it completely omits how the intrinsic reward or the exploration policy $f_i$ is actually computed. The discussion asserts a specific mechanism involving the estimation of state and environment change rarity, and later notes that C-BET relies on (pseudo)counts. Because the formal results provide no equations, lemmas, or definitions for novelty estimation or intrinsic reward calculation, these claims are not established by the provided mathematics. Add the formal definition of the intrinsic reward or state novelty estimator to the results section, or remove the specific claims about rarity estimation and C-BET from the discussion until they are formalized.

comments.title: Behavioral narrowing claim lacks theoretical support
comments.quote: Similarly, transferring policies learned in a related setting –as we do– can help narrow the range of the agent's expected behavior.
comments.explanation: The formal results establish that the pre-trained exploration policy $f_i$ is added as a fixed bias to the task-specific policy $f_e$ during transfer. However, the results contain no theoretical claims, bounds, or proofs regarding the range of the agent's expected behavior or how this additive transfer mechanism constrains it. The discussion asserts a behavioral narrowing effect that is not derived from or supported by the provided policy combination formula or training procedure. Remove this claim from the discussion or provide a formal argument (e.g., a bound on policy divergence or support) to substantiate it.

comments.title: Unclear justification for L2 norm baseline in Figure 4
comments.quote: With the L2 norm of state changes (middle), the agent is biased in favor of moving, because its position is encoded with the highest value in the observation space.
comments.explanation: The explanation provided here is mathematically unsound for standard observation encodings. If the state is represented as a one-hot or categorical vector, the L2 norm of the difference between any two distinct states is constant. Consequently, the L2 norm of state changes would not inherently bias the agent toward moving over interacting with objects. The authors should clarify the exact observation representation used for this baseline or correct the caption, as the current reasoning does not hold. Rewrite the caption to specify the exact encoding used for the baseline, or replace the L2 norm comparison with a count-based baseline that aligns with the paper's methodology.

comments.title: Vague performance claim in abstract
comments.quote: In this context, we evaluate several baseline exploration strategies and present a simple yet effective approach to learning task-agnostic exploration policies. Our key idea is that there are two components of exploration: (1) an agent-centric component encouraging exploration of unseen parts of the environment based on an agent’s belief; (2) an environment-centric component encouraging exploration of inherently interesting objects. We show that our formulation is effective and provides the most consistent exploration across several training-testing environment pairs.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'most consistent exploration' lacks a concrete metric or baseline, making it difficult to evaluate the claim before reading the full paper. Consistency could refer to variance in success rates, sample efficiency, or coverage across environments. To strengthen the abstract, specify the exact metric used to measure consistency or name the primary baseline against which this consistency is demonstrated. This small addition will give readers a clearer expectation of the empirical contribution.
